global_notes:
Pacing sits at ~1.2s/beat average — brisk and firmly music-cut, with a clear weight toward the front of the edit: the 11 "added" beats (vs. 7 "kept" from the base pipeline) tell me this editor layered extra cuts into the opener and prep to build momentum, then let the 9 trims compress the technique stretch so the blending/pounding hits land on downbeats rather than sprawling.

Camera logic is conventional but disciplined — 64% front for the hero/reveal moments (opener, ingredient beauty, final plating) and 36% overhead reserved for where geometry matters (knife work on basil, garlic prep, the emulsification in the bowl); overhead gets clustered during the technique section where top-down reads clearer, then the edit snaps back to front for the transformation reveal.

The rhythm pattern — dense add-ins early, trims through the middle, looser holds at the end — is a classic accel-then-breathe structure: energy ramps through prep, peaks at the blend, then decelerates for the plating payoff so the final dish gets a full musical phrase instead of a beat.

The two `editorial_group` tags read as a deliberate stylized micro-sequence (likely a rapid same-action montage — probably the basil-picking or the pound-and-add cycle) used as a texture moment rather than an info-bearing cut.

Overall: an editor who trusts the music more than the recipe's literal chronology — willing to invent beats to serve rhythm, willing to cut coverage to keep the phrase clean, and parking overhead angles where they actually teach rather than defaulting to them for variety.

beats.beat_description: beauty opener (3 shots): Pesto pouring into glass jar from above → Pesto stream ending into jar → Spaghetti on white plate, pesto dollops at edges

camera.reasoning: Front is the anchor because the vertical pesto stream falling into the jar is the best-take hero moment — the pour's verticality only reads dramatically at eye-level, and overhead is reserved as the switch point at 2.5s to reveal the finished bowl.

duration.reasoning: 3.9s holds long enough for the viewer to read the "Basil Pesto" title card + Kitchn logo across three beauty shots, with the 50% speed ramp stretching the pour itself so the title has time to land before the overhead reveal.

in_point.reasoning: 109.4s catches the pesto mid-fall into a partially-filled jar — the stream is already established and the jar has enough sauce to read as "pesto," avoiding the empty-jar beginning or the static settled-sauce end.

beats.effects_reasoning: The speed ramp is an organic flourish — pesto pouring is a found beautiful transformation that gets slowed to savor the stream, while the punch-in/crop and reframe are functional tools to tighten composition on the jar and correct framing under the title card overlay. [flourish: functional]

camera.reasoning: Front is the anchor/hero angle for the final beauty shot — it shows the bowl's dimensional presentation (parmesan mounded on top, pasta color, bowl rim) the way a viewer would see the finished dish placed in front of them, which overhead would flatten into just a color disc.

duration.reasoning: 4.1s is the closing hold — longer than mid-video beats because the end card needs time to read (Kitchn logo, finished dish), and the 50% speed ramp stretches ~2s of real bowl-slide motion into a slower, more luxurious settle.

in_point.reasoning: 389.1s catches the bowl just as the hand is still at the edge pushing it toward camera — the last bit of motion before the dish comes to rest, giving the shot a "presented to you" arrival rather than starting on a static plate.

beats.effects_reasoning: Speed ramp is functional, not organic — there's no transformation happening (VLM confirms the source is essentially static cheese-on-pasta), so the 50% is purely to slow the presentation gesture and buy hang-time for the end card. [flourish: functional]
